content.body:
**Video** can exist in two forms:

- **Analog video:** a continuous, time-varying electrical signal that represents changing image information
- **Digital video:** a sequence of discrete digital images called **frames**, each a grid of pixel values

Digital video is just a series of still images played fast enough that the eye perceives motion.

The entire frame is drawn **row by row, top to bottom, in a single pass**. Lines are written in order: 1, 2, 3, 4…

- 1 pass = 1 complete frame
- Used in **computer monitors** (typically 72 Hz or higher)
- At high refresh rates, flicker is imperceptible

Each frame is split into **two fields** displayed alternately:

- **Field 1 (odd field):** draws lines 1, 3, 5, 7…
- **Field 2 (even field):** draws lines 2, 4, 6, 8…
- Two fields together = one complete frame

**Why?** Early CRT hardware couldn't send a full frame fast enough to avoid flicker. Sending 60 fields/sec (alternating odd and even) tricks the eye into perceiving smooth motion even though full resolution only updates at 30 fps.

**Example:** NTSC TV at 30 fps interlaced → 60 fields/sec, each field having half the vertical resolution.

After drawing a line, the electron beam **jumps back** from the right end of the line to the left start of the next line. During this jump the beam is **blanked (turned off)** so no visible line is drawn.
After drawing the last line of a field, the beam **jumps from the bottom of the screen back to the top** to start the next field. The beam is also blanked during this jump.

rows: Odd lines first, then even lines
Designed to reduce flicker at low Hz
Combing: horizontal comb-like tearing on fast movement

content.body: Exam tip: interlaced was invented because sending a **full frame** fast enough to avoid flicker was technically difficult. By sending **60 fields/sec** (instead of 30 fps), the eye perceives less flicker — even though each field only has **half** the vertical resolution.

An image stored as a **grid of pixels** (dot matrix). Described by:
- Width × Height in pixels
- Bits per pixel (color depth)

**Resolution dependent** — scaling up causes blurring and pixelation because the computer must estimate new pixels from existing ones.

An image stored as **mathematical descriptions** of shapes — lines, curves, polygons, defined by equations and coordinates.

**Resolution independent** — zooming in always produces a perfect, sharp image because the shapes are redrawn from math at any scale.

content.title: Raster vs Vector — The Circle Comparison
content.body:
**Raster circle:** stored as a grid of pixels approximating the curve. Zoom in → blocky, jagged (pixelated) edges.

**Vector circle:** stored as a mathematical equation (center point + radius). Zoom in → still a perfect, smooth circle. The renderer recalculates where every edge pixel should be at the new scale.

A **sample** is a single measured value at a specific position in space (or time).

In a color image, each pixel contains **multiple samples — one per channel**:
- A **24-bit pixel** has **3 samples**: Red (8 bits), Green (8 bits), Blue (8 bits)
- Each 8-bit sample can take values 0–255

Sampling maps a continuous signal (light intensity) to a discrete set of numbers.

flashcards.q: Why was interlaced scanning invented? What problem did it solve?
flashcards.a: Early hardware couldn't transmit or display a **full frame fast enough** to avoid visible flicker. By sending **60 fields/sec** (alternating odd and even lines) instead of 30 complete frames, the eye perceives smoother motion — even though full vertical resolution only updates 30 times per second.

flashcards.q: What does it mean for interlaced video to show 'combing' artifacts?
flashcards.a: Combing happens with **fast-moving objects**. Because odd and even fields are captured at slightly different moments in time, moving edges appear as **horizontal comb-like stripes** when both fields are viewed together as a still frame.

flashcards.q: Progressive vs interlaced: what's the one-sentence difference?
flashcards.a: Progressive writes **all lines in one pass** (1 field = 1 frame); interlaced writes **odd lines then even lines** in separate passes (2 fields = 1 frame).

flashcards.q: Why does vector graphics scale without quality loss, while raster doesn't?
flashcards.a: Vector images are **stored as math** (equations for shapes). At any size, the renderer calculates pixels from scratch — always perfect. Raster images are **stored as fixed pixel grids** — zoom in and the computer has to guess, producing blur or jagged edges.

flashcards.q: What does 'blanked' mean during retrace, and why does it matter?
flashcards.a: The electron beam is **turned off** during retrace (both horizontal and vertical) so it doesn't draw unwanted lines across the screen. Without blanking, you'd see a bright diagonal streak every time the beam repositioned.

flashcards.q: A pixel is described as '24-bit'. How many samples does it contain, and why?
flashcards.a: It contains **3 samples**: Red (8 bits), Green (8 bits), Blue (8 bits) = 24 bits total. A sample is a single measured value per channel, and a color image has one channel per color component.

problems.q: A TV displays 30 frames per second using interlaced scanning. How many fields per second does it display, and what fraction of lines does each field contain?
problems.a:
**60 fields/sec** — each frame is split into 2 fields (odd + even), so 30 × 2 = 60.

Each field contains **half the vertical lines** — so each field has half the vertical resolution. The eye integrates both fields into a single perceived frame.

problems.q: A designer uses raster graphics to create a company logo, then needs to print it on a billboard. What problem will they face, and what format should they have used?
problems.a:
Raster images are **resolution-dependent**. Scaling up the logo to billboard size will produce **blurring and pixelation** because the software must invent new pixels.

They should have used **vector graphics** — the logo would be stored as mathematical shapes and could be scaled to any size without quality loss.
problems.hint: Think: what is stored differently between raster and vector?

problems.q: In video, what is the difference between a 'sample' and a 'pixel'? Can a pixel contain multiple samples?
problems.a:
A **sample** is a single measured value at one position for one channel. A **pixel** is the complete color unit at one position, which may contain multiple samples.

Yes — a 24-bit RGB pixel contains **3 samples**: R (8 bits), G (8 bits), B (8 bits). Each is an independent measurement of one color channel.
problems.hint: Think channels: RGB means 3 channels per pixel
